narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including scoring a 35 on your LSAT and maintaining a 2.7 undergraduate GPA while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—our goal is to select students with the strongest likelihood of bar passage success. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year and had the strongest pull toward forecasting a bar failure. Your LSAT score, while respectable, fell short of the typical 36.7 average and worked somewhat against you by not pushing the prediction as strongly toward success as higher scores do for others. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, below the dataset average of 3.21, contributed to the risk assessment by signaling slightly lower academic readiness compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 4 also nudged the prediction toward failure, though less intensely than your later performance. On a brighter note, factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans offered minor boosts toward a passing prediction, aligning with patterns seen in successful students. Overall, your profile stands out as having weaker late-stage high school performance relative to the typical applicant, who often ranks in the 5th or 6th decile by Year 3. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to aim above the average, bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses, and focusing on improving your academic decile rankings in any ongoing studies. We encourage you to seek feedback from academic advisors or bar prep programs tailored for students like you, and reapply once you've addressed these key areas—many applicants turn predictions around with targeted improvements. Your determination as a white male from a middle-income background positions you well to overcome this; persistence pays off in law. Keep pushing forward.
